text: The video shows the digital scale displaying the weight of the morselated myoma, which is 3.58 kg. The solid parts of the myoma are visible, and the fluid from the central degeneration is noted. The surgery was performed laparoscopically without opening the abdomen.

The surgeon shows the patient's abdomen at the end of the surgery, which appears flat and without distension. The patient is about to be extubated. The video will now demonstrate the surgical procedure from the beginning, starting with the insertion of the varus needle at Palmer's point.

The surgeon performs an irrigation test, suction test, and hanging drop test before introducing the varus needle through the Palmar's point, located two centimeters above the costal margin, known as Mishra's point. The abdomen is insufflated with carbon dioxide at a pressure of eighteen millimeters of mercury to create more space for the procedure. An 11 mm optical trocar is introduced after enlarging the incision.

The surgeon introduces the first trocar at Palmer's point, navigating carefully due to the abdomen being filled with a large fibroid. The telescope is inserted through the paracolic gutter to avoid the fibroid, and the patient's head is positioned downward. A second port is placed at the anterior axillary line, approximately ten centimeters away from the first port, under direct vision. A third port is introduced along the same line, positioned epigastrically, three centimeters below and left of the xiphoid process, following the baseball diamond concept.

The surgeon positions the ports and uses a grasper in each hand to manipulate the large myoma occupying the abdominal cavity. The patient's head is tilted down and to the right to create space for the telescope, which is positioned at the GP sternum. The fibroid extends above the costal margin, pressing against the cecum and small intestine. The round ligament on the right side is visible, and the fibroid is noted to be pedunculated with torsion.

The surgeon attempts to detort the ovary and fallopian tube, which are visibly twisted due to the presence of a large myoma. The ovary and fallopian tube are manipulated with a grasper, but complete detorsion is challenging due to the size of the myoma. The fallopian tube on the opposite side is also visible. The telescope is repositioned to view the base of the myoma, where an extracorporeal Mishra's knot is applied to the pedicle.

The surgeon changes the telescope position to visualize the pedicle from below. A number one Y-curl suture is encircled around the pedicle, brought out from the opposite side, and tied using an extracorporeal Mishra's knot. The knot is tightened outside the body, while the assistant uses a mama screw to pull up the pedicle for better definition. The round ligament and infundibulopelvic ligament are visible, being pushed towards the upper abdomen.

The surgeon tightens the extracorporeal knot over the pedicle, forming a distinct dumbbell shape. The knot is secured tightly, and the ligature is applied approximately one centimeter above the knot. Both ligature and harmonic devices are used to enucleate and detach the tissue.

The telescope is positioned in the lumbar area, providing a view of the surgical field. The surgeon uses a ligature to coagulate and separate tissue, while carefully managing potential bleeding. A harmonic scalpel from a new company is employed for dissection, gradually separating the tissue.

The surgeon addresses bleeding and oozing from a highly vascular myoma, using a combination of ligature as a vessel sealer and harmonic scalpel as a dissector to carefully separate the myoma from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate and cut the pedicle of the myoma during laparoscopic myomectomy. The vibrating jaw of the harmonic scalpel is positioned upwards to lift and cut the pedicle slowly, ensuring no vessels are left untouched to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon uses an extracorporeal knot to prevent bleeding while slowly cutting through the large myoma. Visible are big blood vessels that have been coagulated, demonstrating the complementary use of energy devices and knotting techniques to manage hemostasis.

The surgeon uses two myoma screws, one inserted through the epigastric port and another from the Palmer's point, to reposition and pull the large myoma over the anterior surface of the liver. The screws provide access for continued myomectomy, as the pedicle, including some uterine tissue, is gradually cut out while the knot secures the area.

The surgeon continues the laparoscopic myomectomy, having cut approximately 75% of the pedicle. The remaining pedicle is being dissected while ensuring the right ovary is protected. The myoma's location allows for successful laparoscopic access, avoiding conversion to open surgery.

During the laparoscopic myomectomy, the surgeon uses a ligature to desiccate the cut edge of the myoma to control oozing. The focus is on achieving hemostasis before detaching the myoma completely to prevent increased bleeding once tension is released.

The surgeon carefully detaches the last part of the myoma pedicle, ensuring hemostasis before complete separation. An instrument is used to elevate the pedicle, preventing damage to underlying structures such as the bowel. The extracorporeal knot is visible, tightly securing the pedicle and forming a dumbbell shape as the final cut is made.

The surgeon ties an additional pre-tied loop, known as Mistra's knot, around the large pedicle of the myoma to reinforce the initial knot and prevent re-bleeding. The knot is tightened securely at the base of the pedicle, ensuring hemostasis before proceeding with morselation. The myoma is positioned near the gallbladder for the next step.

The surgeon identifies and removes a para-ovarian cyst located on the left side of the ovary. The infundibulo-ovarian ligament is cut to facilitate removal. The fallopian tube on the same side is also excised, leaving a six-millimeter segment near the uterus.

The surgeon completes the ovarian cystectomy and prepares for cholecystectomy and appendicectomy. A morselator is used to morselate the tube and cyst, extracting them from the abdomen. The myoma is positioned in the epigastric region for morselation, with the telescope in the palmers and the myoma screw in the lumbar area to facilitate the process. Due to the large size of the myoma, the entire structure is not visible at once, requiring continuous adjustment of the telescope for panoramic viewing during morselation.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic morselation of the myoma, gradually reducing its size by fragmenting the tissue. The morselation process is shown intermittently, highlighting the removal of tissue fragments from the surgical field.

The surgeon performs cholecystectomy with the telescope positioned in the lumbar area and instruments in the Palmer's point and epigastric region, deviating from standard port placement. Despite the challenging ergonomics, the gallbladder is being carefully manipulated and dissected.

The surgeon uses a port with the telescope positioned in the lumbar area to create a posterior peritoneal window by applying anteromedial traction. The dissection occurs above the Rouviere's sulcus, separating the posterior peritoneum from the left side. The critical view of safety is established, showing two structures leading to the gallbladder and the visible inferior surface of the liver. The artery and vein are identified, with a window between them, while the left lobe of the liver obstructs the view due to the telescope's position.

The surgeon separates the posterior peritoneum, followed by stripping the anterior peritoneum. A window is created between the artery and duct, with the inferior surface of the liver visible below. The common bile duct (CBD) is identified, and the procedure is performed above the Rouvier's sulcus.

The surgeon uses a clip applicator at Palmer's point to perform a cholecystectomy, focusing on the porta hepatis at the level of the Rouvière's sulcus. The posterior window is enlarged to ensure the inferior surface of the liver is clearly visible, achieving a critical view of safety. The gallbladder is separated from the liver through posterior peritoneal dissection, with further enlargement of the anterior and posterior windows and skeletonization of the structures.

The surgeon enlarges the anterior window to ensure clear visibility of the cystic duct and artery. The duct is positioned below the artery, which is in front, both structures are clearly visible. A titanium clip is prepared for application.

A titanium clip is applied over the cystic duct, avoiding lateral clipping due to its dilation. The common hepatic duct is visible in the foreground. A single clip is applied to the artery, followed by cutting the artery using a harmonic scalpel for coagulation. The cystic duct is then cut using the same technique.

The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to separate the posterior peritoneum, followed by anterolateral traction to cut the right shoulder of the gallbladder, facilitating its separation from the liver. Despite ergonomic challenges due to multiple surgeries, the gallbladder is being carefully extracted.

The surgeon completes the removal of the gallbladder, concluding the cholecystectomy. Blood is visible in the upper abdomen, attributed to the myomectomy procedure, and is being suctioned out. The surgical team checks the myoma pedicle, confirming it is intact and without issues.

The surgeon lifts the appendix and performs a laparoscopic appendectomy using a ligature harmonic device. The gallbladder is also held in place. The mesoappendix is cooked and pulled towards the cecum, with the ileum visible below. The appendix is mobilized from the lateral peritoneum, and the major appendix is cut at its base near the tinea coli to ensure complete removal.

The surgeon uses a pre-tied Misra knot, placing it at the base of the appendix and securing it with a knot pusher. The knot is tightened and cut using a harmonic scalpel, sealing and cauterizing the mucosa. The appendix and gallbladder are removed through a 15mm morcellator port. The surgical field is inspected for collected blood in the cul-de-sac, which is then suctioned out.

The surgeon performs final suction of blood collected in the cul-de-sac, inspecting the right ureter and sacral promontory. The pararectal area is checked for fragmented myoma tissue, with visible blood present. The ureter is relieved from pressure due to myoma removal. The surgeon prepares for port closure, ensuring the 15mm port is closed while the Palmer's port remains open above the costal margin.

The surgeon uses a cobbler's needle to perform port closure, ensuring all layers except the skin are included in the suture to prevent incisional hernia. The abdomen appears lax after the removal of a large fibroid, resembling a full-term pregnancy at the start of the procedure.
